title: Introductory. Where to start in trading? Part 1
subtitle: Spot vs futures, safe start, risk before the first trade
outcome: Explain spot and futures, put together a registration checklist and name the main risk of a newbie.
content: Trading is buying and selling for profit. Start: spot, KYC, 2FA, deposit risk before click.
takeaway: Start spot; futures = leverage and liquidation; safety and risk first, then warrants.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
Trading - active trading: bought cheaper / sold more expensive (long) or vice versa (short on futures).

Spot: if you buy a coin for USDT, it’s yours. No leverage, no liquidation. Fall = loss on the position, but will not be “knocked out” automatically.

Futures: price contract with leverage. Profit/loss is faster, but liquidation is possible. For a beginner, the first weeks are only spot and micro-sized.

sections.title: Spot vs futures: different risk
sections.caption: Spot: risk ≤ position size. Futures: Leverage compresses the stock until liquidation.

sections.body:
Exchange (Binance / Bybit, etc.): email → password → KYC (passport/selfie) → 2FA (Google Authenticator or equivalent).

A separate post office for the exchange is better than the main one. Seed/2FA codes - not in Telegram "Favorites".
P2P: buy USDT from a person; check limits, reviews, translation network. After purchase: Wallet → Transfer → Spot, if the money is in Funding.

KYC is required for normal trading and API. This is one step, not a topic for ten charts.

sections.title: Risk thinking before the first trade

sections.body:
Before clicking Buy, record how much you are willing to lose on training (training budget, not “everything you have”).
The rate rule is further: usually 0.5–1% of the deposit per transaction if the stop is triggered.

The terminal (chart + orders) speeds up execution, but does not make the transaction smarter. First, a clear plan on paper, then an order.

sections.title: First risk, then size
sections.caption: The price always moves. Your control is size and stop, don’t “guess the direction.”

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Training deposit 500 USDT. Risk per trade 1% = 5 USDT. Until you know how to calculate the size from the stop (lesson 18), do not increase the deposit “to return it faster.” Futures ×10 for the same 500 turns −10% of the price into an almost complete loss of margin.

sections.title: Remember
items: Training budget and risk limit before clicking

sections.title: Practice
sections.body: No deals - just a checklist:
items: Write down the spot/futures difference in one paragraph
Check: mail, KYC, 2FA - done or planned
Set your training budget in USDT
Record the risk of 1% of this budget in USDT
Find where on the Transfer Funding exchange → Spot

title: Introductory. Where to start? Part 2
subtitle: Limit vs market, maker/taker, risk 0.5–1% and position size
outcome: Explain the limit/market and calculate the size of the risk and stop.
content: Funding≠Spot; the limit is waiting in the glass, the market takes it immediately; size = risk÷stop distance.
takeaway: First transfer to Spot, then limit+stop; risk 0.5–1% → size, not “by eye”.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
USDT often remains in Funding after purchase. The Spot terminal is visible only to Spot Wallet.
Wallet → Transfer → Funding → Spot. Otherwise Buy is grey.

Limit = “no more expensive than X” - you get into a glass, often maker (cheaper).
Market = “now” - take away someone else’s limits, taker + spread.
If it doesn’t light up, learn with a limit.

sections.title: The limit is waiting, the market hits immediately
sections.caption: The risk in USDT is fixed; wider feet - fewer coins.

sections.body:
USDT Risk = Deposit × 0.5–1%.
Size (coins) = USDT Risk ÷ (Entry − Stop) in USDT per coin.
“Order 3–5% of deposit” is a ceiling on the amount, not a replacement for the formula.

sections.title: Stop sets the size
sections.caption: Candle: open/close body, high/low wicks - place a stop behind the structure.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 1000, risk 1% = 10 USDT. ETH entry 3000, stop 2940 (−60$). Size = 10÷60 ≈ 0.167 ETH ≈ 500 USDT face value.

title: Movement amplitude
subtitle: ATR and percentages: BTC vs alts before stop selection
outcome: Measure the amplitude/ATR at 15m and select a stop for BTC or alt.
content: Amplitude - how much the price usually moves per bar/session. Without it, stop and take are random.
takeaway: BTC and alts are different scales; ATR(14) at 15m sets a reasonable stop size.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Amplitude - the scope of the movement: high−low bar or move per session in %.
ATR(14) at 15m - average true range for 14 bars: “typical noise” now.

A stop less than ATR will almost always be disrupted by noise. Stop = 5× ATR without resizing inflates the risk in USDT.

sections.title: Wide and narrow candles
sections.caption: High bars/rising ATR = fast market. Narrow bars = compression.

sections.body:
BTC at 15m often moves a fraction of a percent per bar; thin viola - a few% for the same candle.
The same “$30” stop on ETH and on shitcoin has a different meaning: on alt, $30 can be inside one candle.

First look at the ATR and average % of the bar, then the entry.

sections.title: Same stop ≠ same meaning
sections.caption: On a volatile alt, the bar is wider: the stop should breathe, the position size should be smaller.

sections.body:
Working scheme: stop ≈ 1–1.5× ATR behind the structure (per swing), not a “round number”.
USDT Risk = Deposit × 0.5–1%. Size = USDT risk ÷ (entry − stop).
Higher ATR → wider stops → fewer coins for the same risk.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 2000 USDT, risk 1% = 20 USDT. BTC: ATR(14) 15m ≈ $80. Stop for swing = $120 (≈1.5 ATR). Size = 20 ÷ 120 ≈ 0.167 BTC risk denomination (then cut to the available balance). An alt with an ATR of 4% at a price of $1 and a stop of $0.06 gives a size of 20 ÷ 0.06 ≈ 333 coins - the denomination is different, the risk in USDT is the same 20.

sections.title: Remember
items: Alts are wider than BTC on the same TF

sections.body: On history, without a deal:
items: Record ATR now and average high−low for 5 bars
Open a liquid alt (SOL/ETH) - compare ATR in %
Come up with a stop of ≈1.5 ATR and a risk of 1% of 1000 USDT
Calculate the size using the formula

title: Loyal support
subtitle: Swing loys, zone, false breakout
outcome: Build support for 2+ loys and distinguish a false breakout from a level break.
content: Support is the zone where buyers have already stopped the fall. Touch and reaction are needed.
takeaway: 2+ loya, zone not line; Confirm a false breakout by closing the TF candle.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
A swing-loy is a local minimum, after which the price went up by at least a few bars.
Loyal support: two or more close minimums. Draw a zone (stripe), not a thin line for one tick.

Strength increases with the number of touches and the amount of reaction. One level = not yet level.

sections.title: Support as a zone by loys
sections.caption: Two+ touches from below. Think entry is at the zone, stop - behind the zone.

sections.body:
False breakout: the shadow pierced the zone downwards, but the candle of the selected TF closed back above the support.
Break: closing the TF below the zone and holding / new below low.

A beginner places a stop exactly on the line - it is often removed as a shadow. Stop - behind the zone with a margin under ATR, the size is at risk.

sections.title: Shadow puncture ≠ scrap
sections.caption: See the close of the TF bar. A shadow below the zone with a close above is often a stop trap.

sections.body:
The price has approached the zone → compression/rebound with lower shadows → limit entry in the zone or upon confirmation of close back higher.
Stop below the zone. The target is the nearest resistance or 1.5–2R.
There is no reaction from the zone - there is no input “because the line”.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: ETH 15m: levels 3240 and 3245 → zone 3238–3248. Entry 3250, stop 3225 ($25). Risk 1% with a deposit of 1000 = 10 USDT → size = 10÷25 = 0.4 ETH. Target 1.5R = 3250 + 37.5 ≈ 3288. Puncture to 3230 with close 3252 - false; scrapped - close 3220.

sections.title: Remember
items: Stop outside the zone, not on the line

sections.title: Practice
sections.body: Mark without clicking:
items: BTC or ETH 15m/1H - find a zone of 2-3 loys
Mark one false puncture with a shadow (if there was one)

title: Order book in detail
subtitle: How to read Binance order book: bid, ask, spread, walls
outcome: You will recognize a Binance-style order book and what bid, ask, spread and walls mean.
content: Beginner lesson: order book deep dive.
takeaway: Process beats one lucky trade on Order book deep dive.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.title: What the Binance order book looks like

sections.body:
The order book is a queue of limit orders.

Top = red asks (sellers).
Bottom = green bids (buyers).
Middle = last price and spread.

Chart = trades that already happened. Book = who is waiting right now.

sections.title: Example: BTC/USDT book like Binance
sections.caption: Asks on top (red), bids below (green). Trades happen in the middle.

sections.body:
Best bid = highest buy price.
Best ask = lowest sell price.
Spread = ask − bid.

On liquid BTC the spread is tiny. On thin alts it can be large — market orders become expensive.

sections.title: Example: wide spread on an alt
sections.caption: Yellow band = spread. A market order can burn through several levels.

sections.body:
A wall is a very large size on one price level.

An ask wall is heavy selling interest. Price may bounce… or the wall may cancel.
Rule: never trade only because of a wall — wait for tape and price reaction.

sections.title: Example: ask wall ~48 BTC
sections.caption: Gold WALL tag = abnormal size. Density, not a guaranteed reversal.

sections.body: A bid wall is large buy interest below price. It may hold a drop — or disappear when price approaches.

Spoofing: place a huge limit, scare the market, cancel before fill.

Clues: wall appears far from mid, vanishes on approach, no prints on the tape.
Beginners do not enter “because of the wall”.

Workflow: chart level (15m) → book at that level → tape confirmation.
If chart says support but asks are pressing — wait.

sections.title: Readable book checklist
sections.caption: Best bid/ask → spread → any wall → what the tape shows.

sections.body:
Remember four things: bid / ask / spread / walls.
Practice: watch BTCUSDT book for 15 minutes and log spread + one wall.

sections.title: Practice
sections.body: 20–30 minutes. No live trades:
items: Write best bid, best ask, spread
Find one large wall if present
Watch 10 minutes: did the wall hold or cancel?

sections.title: Scenario
sections.body: Tight spread, ask wall at 67205 for 48 BTC. Price approaches — wall cancels. Not a blind short. Wait for tape + candle.

title: Fast and slow market
subtitle: Market mode and position size
outcome: In 20 seconds, name the mode and decide: full size / −50% / pause.
content: Fast = wide candles/ATR↑; slow = narrow range. In a storm, cut to size.
takeaway: Fast → size −30–50%; slow → don’t click out of boredom; before the news - 0 transactions.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Quick: wide candles, price jumps, news/liquidations.
Slow: narrow corridor, you want “at least something.”
Error: in quick - full size “in time”; in slow mode - entry from boredom.

sections.title: Narrow range → impulse
sections.caption: First, calm candles, then wide candles - the regime changed.

sections.body:
ATR 15m is noticeably higher than usual → fast: size −30–50%, take closer.
The spread is suddenly wider → not the market “into the fire”.
After <30 min CPI/FOMC → do not enter.
The risk per trade is still 0.5–1% - the stop is just wider → fewer coins.

title: Basic mistakes of newbies. Part 1
subtitle: Plan before click, risk 0.5–1%, magazine instead of revenge
outcome: Name 5 common mistakes and replace them with a checklist before entering.
content: Most of the drains are discipline: no stop, FOMO, all-in, revenge, trading without a journal.
takeaway: Stop and risk% before click; log after; revenge and signal chats are outside the system.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.title: Five mistakes that are worth your deposit

sections.body:
1) There is no stop on the stock exchange - “I’ll take it out later.” Result: −10–30% instead of −1%.
2) All-in / risk >2–3% per idea - one series and a month of training is reset to zero.
3) FOMO: mid-pulse input without level and R:R.
4) Revenge: immediately a new entry after the stop to “recapture”.
5) VIP signals / other people's inputs without their own plan and size.

Overtrading is a consequence of 3–4: many trades without a setup.

sections.title: Without a stop, the risk is unlimited
sections.caption: With a stop, the loss is ≈ planned R. Without a stop, as long as you have enough nerve or margin.

sections.body:
On paper (or in a note) before the order:
level/idea → entry → stop → target → risk % of deposit → size.
No item - no click.

After the stop: pause. The minimum is to close the terminal for 15–30 minutes and write down the emotion. Not “another one, now for sure.”

sections.title: First a plan, then an order
sections.caption: The schedule is waiting. Impulse without a plan will run away - and it’s okay to miss it.

sections.body:
Journal: pair, TF, setup, entry/stop/target, R:R, fact of execution, emotion 1–5.
After 20 trades, recurring holes are visible (FOMO, early exit, sitting out).
The terminal does not replace the log: click without recording = no learning.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 1000. Three trades without a stop in a row at −4%, −5%, −6% ≈ −150 USDT (−15%). The same ideas with a stop and a risk of 1%: maximum −30 USDT for three stops. The difference is not a “strategy”, but a loss limit.

title: Descending triangle
subtitle: Flat support + lower highs
outcome: Spot a descending triangle, compute the measured move, and not confuse it with a wedge.
content: Descending triangle: compression into flat support, often before a bearish break.
takeaway: Short only after a close below support (often + retest). Never from mid-triangle.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
A descending triangle is compression:
• nearly flat support below (a zone, not a perfect line);
• a sequence of lower highs above.

Buyers bounce weaker; sellers press earlier. Often a bearish continuation — but you only trade the confirmed breakout side.

Do not confuse with a falling wedge: a wedge has both borders sloping down; a descending triangle has a flat floor.

sections.title: Pattern animation
sections.caption: Flat support + descending highs. Break is down through the floor.

sections.title: Candlestick example
sections.caption: Support touches ~100, lower highs 108→105→103→102, break, retest, target 92.

sections.body:
Trigger: working-TF candle close below support, ideally volume ≥1.5× average.
Entry: on break (aggressive) or retest from below (conservative).
Stop: above the retest or last lower high inside the pattern.
Target (measured move): H = highest high − support; project H down from the breakout.
Risk 0.5–1% of equity; size from the stop distance.

sections.title: Break + retest + target
sections.caption: Schematic: entry, stop beyond the pattern, measured move down.

sections.body:
• Shorting mid-pattern “because it looks like one” — no edge.
• Confusing triangle with wedge.
• Ignoring BTC: a bitcoin pump often kills alt shorts.
• Shorting a wick below support without a close — fakeout / stop hunt.

sections.body:

Support: 100 (3+ touches).
Highs: 108 → 105 → 103 → 102.
H = 108 − 100 = 8.
Close below 100 → short; stop 102.5 (above last LH); target 100 − 8 = 92.
Risk 0.7% of equity. Mark + journal first — click only if the plan still matches.

title: Ascending triangle
subtitle: Flat resistance + higher lows
outcome: Spot an ascending triangle and plan a long after a close above resistance.
content: Ascending triangle: buyers pressing into a flat ceiling.
takeaway: Long after close above resistance, often + retest from above. Never mid-pattern.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
An ascending triangle mirrors the descending one:
• flat resistance above (ceiling / zone);
• higher lows below.

Buyers raise the floor while sellers defend the ceiling — until a close holds above.

Do not confuse with a rising wedge: a wedge slopes both borders up; an ascending triangle has a flat ceiling.

sections.title: Pattern animation
sections.caption: Flat ceiling + rising lows. Break is up through resistance.

sections.body:
≥3 touches of one resistance zone and ≥3 higher lows on one TF. Compression 12–30+ candles.
On ETH/BTC in a bullish 1H/4H context continuation odds improve — but entry still needs a close.

sections.title: Candlestick example
sections.caption: Resistance 64, HL 54→58→60→62, break, retest, target 74 (H=10).

sections.body:
Trigger: close above resistance, preferably with volume.
Entry on break or retest from above (old resistance → support).
Stop: under the retest / last higher low.
Target: H = resistance − lowest low of the pattern; project +H from the breakout.
Risk 0.5–1%.

sections.body:

• Treating a wick above the ceiling as a break without a close.

title: Strategy for impulses
subtitle: Range → breakdown → retest → entry with 1R
outcome: Go through the impulse checklist and name the entry/stop/target in prices.
content: Impulse after compression: wait for close outside and retest, not mid-shot.
takeaway: Login on retest of a broken border; stop for structure; target ≥1.5R; risk 0.5–1%.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
First, a narrow range (8–12+ 15m candles), then closing BEYOND the border.
A newbie buys a mid shot. Plan: wait for the old border to be retested.

sections.title: Breakdown and return
sections.caption: The entry is not into the shot, but at the retest of the border.

sections.body:
1) Range at 15m.
2) Closing abroad (not just shadow).
3) Volume at the breakout is above average.
4) The retest is held by the body.
5) Entry limited by touch.
6) Stop for the retest minimum; target ≥1.5R.

1R = |input − stop| in USDT per coin. Position risk = 0.5–1% of the deposit.

sections.title: Size from 1R
sections.caption: USDT Risk ÷ 1R = size; target at 1.5–2R.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 800, risk 1% = 8 USDT. Entry 100, stop 98 → 1R=2$. Size=4 coins. Target 1.5R=103.

title: Delisting of coins from the exchange
subtitle: Announcement → exit checklist, without catching a knife
outcome: You will go through the exit checklist after the delisting announcement.
content: Delisting - removal of a pair. After the announcement, liquidity melts; the task is to save USDT, not to “beat it off”.
takeaway: Read Announcements; exit in USDT before the deadline; do not catch the rebound and do not average.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
The exchange removes the pair (for example ALT/USDT) from trading by the date in the announcement.
After the news there is often a sharp drop: entering the stable, the spread is wider, the glass is thinner.

The purpose of the lesson is the exit procedure, not a new “support” long.

sections.title: Seller pressure after announcement
sections.caption: Typical: gap down / LL series. Rebounds are often weak.

sections.body:

5) Leverage and averaging are prohibited in the delisting training rule.

title: Listing of a new coin
subtitle: First hours: observation is more important than clicking
outcome: Describe the first day of listing risk and the reduced risk rule.
content: Listing = chaos of spread and volume. First observation; transaction with reduced risk only.
takeaway: Hour 0–1 – watch; idea risk 0.25–0.5%; stop is required; skipping is the norm.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.title: Why is the first hour dangerous?

sections.body:
New pair: the spread is wide, the candles are huge, the glass is jumping.
A common scenario: pump → deep rollback. Buying high gives −20…−50% per session.

Pre-market outside your exchange ≠ your executed price. The reference point is the facts of the order book and the chart after you open a pair.

sections.title: Pump and rollback after opening
sections.caption: The first wave is often the most chaotic. The structure comes later.

sections.body:
Risk per listing idea: 0.25–0.5% of deposit (less than usual 1%).
0–60 min: observation only - high/low, spread, glass speed.
After 3–24 hours: You can mark the level on paper.
Trade (if needed): stop immediately, without averaging, without “catch up”.

sections.title: Size smaller than usual
sections.caption: The same stop in % on chaos is knocked out more often - so we reduce the risk in USDT.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 2000, risk 0.5% = 10 USDT. Entry 1.00, stop 0.92 (0.08). Size = 10 ÷ 0.08 = 125 coins. Buying a market “at the high” of 1.40 without a stop with a rollback to 0.90 = −~36% for the position is incomparable with the training limit of 10 USDT.

title: Channels: parallel support and resistance
subtitle: Long from channel support, short from resistance
outcome: Build a channel and name the entry/stop/target from the borders to the breakout.
content: A channel is a corridor of parallel boundaries. In range, people trade from edge to edge, not from the middle.
takeaway: Long at channel support, short at resistance; stop abroad; after the breakdown - another logic.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Parallel lines: the bottom line is along 2+ swing loys, the top line is parallel to the highs (or vice versa: the top is along the highs, the bottom is parallel).
Need repeated touches. Two points “by eye” without reaction is a weak channel.

Instrument: Equidistant Channel / parallel lines on TradingView or exchange.

sections.title: Price channel
sections.caption: Floor = support, ceiling = resistance. Trade at the borders.

sections.title: Long from support / short from resistance

sections.body:
In a sideways / stable channel:
• Long at the lower border: stop below the channel (for the last swing / ATR stock), the target is the middle or upper border.
• Short at the top: stop above the channel, target - middle or bottom.

The middle of the channel is a zone of uncertainty: worse than R:R, more often saw. In a strong one-sided trend, the counter-border (shorts in an uptrend at the “upper”) - be more careful.

sections.title: Entrance from the border, not from the center
sections.caption: Stop behind the channel. A close breakout beyond the border cancels the range scenario.

sections.body:
Close of the selected timeframe abroad + hold = end of range logic.
Next: either wait for a retest of the border as a new support/resistance, or do not trade “to catch up”.
False breakout: taking it outside and close back inside - often a return to trading from the edge.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: ETH channel 1H: bottom 3120–3130, top 3280–3290. Long from 3135, stop 3105 ($30), target 3280 (≈$145). R:R ≈ 1:4.8 to top; partial target mid 3205 ≈ 1:2.3. Short from 3285, stop 3315 ($30), target 3130. Entry 3200 “because in the middle” with a stop of $30 to the top gives a target down below the edge - a weak plan.

sections.title: Remember
items: Long from the bottom of the channel
Short from the top of the channel
Close per channel = change of scenario

title: Uptrend: Higher High and Higher Low
subtitle: HH/HL structure and long pullback
outcome: See HH/HL and describe an entry on a pullback to HL with a stop.
content: An uptrend is a series of higher highs (HH) and higher lows (HL).
takeaway: Buy pullbacks to HL, don't chase every HH; HL's breakdown by closing calls the trend into question.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Higher High (HH) - a high higher than the previous high.
Higher Low (HL) - the minimum of the retracement above the previous low.
Chain HH → HL → HH → HL = uptrend is alive.

Broken: close TF below the last HL - the structure is broken until a new HL appears.

sections.title: Staircase HH/HL
sections.caption: Each pullback is higher than the last. Think long at HL, not at the peak of HH.

sections.body:
Basic scenario: wait for a rollback to the HL zone / local support → reaction → long.
Stop below HL (with ATR margin). Target - previous HH or 1.5-2R / new HH.

Buying on HH itself “so as not to miss out” often gives a worse R:R and a stop for the entire impulse.

sections.title: Entry on rollback to HL
sections.caption: HL = structure support. Stop - under it.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: BTC 15m: HH 68200, HL 67650, new HH 68450. Rollback to 67720. Entry 67750, stop 67580 ($170). 1R = 170. Target 1.5R = 67750 + 255 = 68005; to the past HH 68450 ≈ 2R+. Chasing 68440 with a stop of 67580 gives R a huge downside with little reward upside.

sections.title: Remember
items: Close under HL = risk of breaking

items: ETH or BTC 15m/1H – check 2 HH and 2 HL

title: Downtrend: Lower High and Lower Low
subtitle: LH/LL, short from structure, stop above LH
outcome: See LH/LL and short the bounce to LH with a stop above LH.
content: A downtrend is a series of lower highs (LH) and lower lows (LL).
takeaway: Don't catch the knife from LL; short from LH after structure, stop above LH; at the spot, often just stand to the side.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Lower High (LH) - a rebound upward, the high is lower than the previous high.
Lower Low (LL) - a new low lower than the previous one.
Chain LH → LL → LH → LL = downward trend.

The downward trend is alive until the close TF consolidates above the last LH. An upward breakout of LH is the first signal of a break in the bearish structure.

sections.title: Ladder LH/LL
sections.caption: Every bounce is lower. Short with LH, not buy at the bottom with LL.

sections.body:
Futures/short scenario: after LL, the price rebounds to the LH zone (or slightly lower) → reaction of sellers → short.
Stop – above LH (beyond the rebound maximum / ATR reserve). Target - previous LL or 1.5-2R / new LL.

On the spot without a short: in a downtrend they often don’t buy “because -15% already happened.” Wait for LH to break or trade other pairs.

sections.title: Short from LH, stop higher
sections.caption: 1R = stop above LH minus entry. Aim down to LL/1.5–2R.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: ETH 15m downtrend: LH 3480, LL 3390, new bounce to 3465 (new LH below 3480). Short 3460, stop 3495 (above LH, $35). 1R = 35. Target 1.5R = 3460 − 52.5 ≈ 3407; to LL 3390 ≈ 2R. Buy at 3395 and “bounce” with a stop far below - against the structure.

sections.title: Remember
items: Don't catch LL with a knife
Close above LH = risk of downtrend break

title: MA, RSI and volume: three momentum filters
subtitle: Consent filters, not the holy trinity of signals
outcome: Filter the entry by MA + RSI + volume and refuse if one filter is against it.
content: MA - trend context, RSI - overheating/rollback, volume - confirmation. Together = filter, not grail.
takeaway: Three filters agree - we can consider the input at the level; one against - wait. Indicators do not cancel stops.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
MA 20 / MA 50: price above both - bullish context; below is bearish. A rollback to MA 20 in an uptrend is an area of ​​interest, not an auto-buy.

RSI(14): >70 is not equal to “short”, <30 is not equal to “long”; in a strong trend, the oscillator can remain at the edges for a long time. More useful: RSI returning from overheating to 45–55 on a pullback.

Volume: impulse candle/breakout with above average volume (≈20 bars) is stronger. A breakdown on a quiet volume is a suspicion.

sections.title: MA Context and Volume Momentum
sections.caption: Indicators confirm the idea at the level. Without level - line noise.

sections.body:
Long filter (example): price above MA 50 → rollback to MA 20 / level → RSI does not wedge in the short idea (upward reversal from the zone 40–50) → bullish candle with volume ≥ average.
The short filter is mirrored below MA 50.

If the MA says uptrend, and you short only “RSI 72”, the filters do not agree. Pass.
MA+RSI+Volume does not replace stops and size from the risk of 0.5–1%.

sections.title: First level and trend, then filters
sections.caption: Three “yes” for HL/support are stronger than three lines in the void.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Long ETH: price above MA 50, rollback to MA 20 ≈ 3320 (support zone there). The RSI went to 42 and turned around. Entry 3325 at above average volume. Stop 3295 ($30). With a risk of 15 USDT, size = 0.5 ETH. The same RSI 42 below MA 50 without a level - there is no trend filter, the lesson setup is cancelled.

items: Find 1 place where all three filters are “for” long at the level

title: CPI and FOMC news: trading around macro
subtitle: Calendar, volatility, rules before and after release
outcome: Make a risk plan for the CPI/FOMC window and explain why you are not trading blindly into an event.
content: CPI and FOMC are moving the dollar and are risk-active; crypto often inherits volatility. Trade - according to the rules of the window.
takeaway: 15 minutes before release risk↓ or flat; after - wait for the spread/ATR to stabilize, do not catch the first tick.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
CPI - US inflation index. A strong deviation from the forecast moves expectations for the Federal Reserve rate.
FOMC rate decision and press conference. Often there are two impulses: the rate decision itself and speech.

BTC/ETH at such moments inherit the risk-on/risk-off: ATR soars, the spread is wider, stops are removed more often.

sections.title: Event window on the timeline
sections.caption: Before / release / after. The most dangerous period is the minutes around the publication.

sections.body:
Calendar (Forex Factory / Fed website / news section) - time in your time zone.
15–30 minutes before: Reduce size by half or close training positions; do not open new “guessing games”.
At the tick: not market “in the first candle”. Spread and slippage break R:R.
After 5–15 minutes: evaluate the new range/direction, return the size only if the ATR is clear and there is a level.

For a beginner, leverage on the macro window is an extra factor to the noise.

sections.title: Stop and size for ATR surge
sections.caption: The same $ stop after CPI is often too tight. Either wider feet + smaller size, or flat.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 3000, normal risk 1% = 30 USDT. 20 minutes before CPI: either flat or risk 0.5% = 15 USDT. ATR 15m before news 60$; after the impulse of $140. A “like yesterday” stop of $80 with a risk of 30 USDT gives a size of 0.375 - but the probability of the stop being taken out has increased. Rational: wait close 15m after the release and a new HL/LH, do not click on the 1st minute.

title: Deposit risk: 0.5–1% per transaction
subtitle: Position size from stop, not by eye
outcome: Calculate the position size from the risk of 0.5–1% and the distance to the stop.
content: Risk per trade - how much % of the deposit you will lose if the stop is triggered. The size is derived from the stop.
takeaway: First stop and risk% - then size. Leverage does not change the risk limit in USDT.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
USDT risk = deposit × 0.005…0.01.
This is the maximum loss in theory, if the stop is executed approximately according to plan (plus slippage - add a margin).

The position value may be greater than the risk: you risk only a piece up to the stop, not the entire order amount (on spot, the loss is limited to a fall to the stop/sell).

sections.title: Fixed risk, different size
sections.caption: Narrow stop → more coins. Wide stop → fewer coins. The risk of USDT is the same.

sections.body:
Size (coins) = USDT risk ÷ |entry − stop| in USDT per coin.

Leverage changes the margin, but the training risk% should remain 0.5–1% of the deposit: not “risked 1% of the margin at ×20”, but 1% of the entire account before the stop.
A series of 5–10 stops in a row at 1% is unpleasant, but there is no count; at 5–10% per transaction - often the end of training.

sections.title: Stop sets the denominator
sections.caption: Without the price of a foot, the size formula does not exist.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 1500 USDT, risk 1% = 15 USDT. Long SOL entry 145, stop 141.5 ($3.5). Size = 15 ÷ 3.5 ≈ 4.29 SOL ≈ denomination 622 USDT. The same stop at a risk of 0.5% → the size is half as large. “Buy for all 1500 without a stop” - the risk is up to −100% of the deposit on the idea, not 1%.

items: Series of small stops > one all-in

title: Symmetric triangle
subtitle: Squeeze → break. Measured move with numbers
outcome: Mark a symmetric triangle and compute the 1:1 target from height H.
content: A symmetric triangle is balance before the break; do not guess the direction early.
takeaway: Wait for a close beyond the border + volume; target ≈ H from the breakout.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Symmetric triangle: lower highs and higher lows — a “coiling spring”.
Both borders slope and converge toward the apex; they must not cross upside-down.

Do not guess direction early: trade the close side.
A wick beyond the border ≠ breakout — you need a working-TF candle close.

sections.title: Symmetric triangle
sections.caption: Two converging lines. Wait for a close beyond the border.

sections.body:
1) Measure height H at the widest (base) part of the triangle.
2) From the breakout price (close beyond the border) project H in the breakout direction — 1:1 target.
3) Stop beyond the opposite border or last swing inside the pattern.

Example: H = 120. Upside break close 5400. Target ≈ 5400 + 120 = 5520.

sections.title: Pattern height = target size
sections.caption: H at the base; target = break ± H. Lines converge, they do not cross wrong-way.

sections.body:
BNBUSDT 4H.
Upper border (LH): 620 → 600 → 590.
Lower border (HL): 560 → 570 → 580.
Base H ≈ 620 − 560 = 60.

4H close above the upper line near 592–595 → upside break.
Entry 593, stop 579 (under last HL / lower border), R ≈ 14.
Measured target 592 + 60 = 652.
Scale out at 1.5R (≈ 614) and trail the rest — full 1:1 is often too far for intraday.

sections.title: Symmetric triangle breakout
sections.caption: Close beyond the border + stop + target from H.

sections.title: Numbers walkthrough
sections.body:
H = 40, upside break close 100, entry 100.5, stop 97.5 (R = 3), measured target 140.
Even a partial 1.5R = 105 beats a random entry. No close beyond the border — no setup.

title: FUN and meme tokens: risks and momentum
subtitle: DOGE as an example of a volatility narrative
outcome: You will understand how memes differ from BTC, and how to trade momentum without FOMO.
content: Meme tokens are driven by narrative and crowd - higher volatility and slippage.
takeaway: Small size, hard stop, quick fixation - not an “investment for years.”
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
Meme tokens (DOGE, FUN and analogues) live from hype on social networks, and not from the foundation like BTC.
The spread is wider, the candles are longer, the rollback is −30…−50% within hours - a normal picture.

Before any meme idea, open BTC 15m/1H: if BTC falls, the meme almost always falls more. No BTC context - no entry.

sections.title: Meme impulse on BTC background
sections.caption: First the BTC direction, then the meme-momentum.

sections.body:
Risk per meme trade: 0.25–0.5% of the deposit (half the usual).
Stop - always, hard: behind the low of the impulse candle / behind the trigger entry level. Not “I’ll wait longer.”

Take: partial fixation early (1R), rest as planned. Don’t turn the scalp meme into hold “because everyone is buying.”

sections.title: Low risk, short stop

title: Holding to the goal: take profit discipline
subtitle: Don't close early out of fear and greed
outcome: Learn to hold the position to the plan, fix it partially and not move the stop against yourself.
content: The goal is set before entering - deviation from it without reason destroys R:R.
takeaway: Hold until the goal or partial takes according to the plan - not to the point of “being afraid to give away your profit.”
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.title: The goal is written before the click

sections.body:
Before entry: entry, stop, target (minimum 1.5–2R). This is the plan.
After entering, the brain wants to “close +0.3R - that’s already a plus.” This is how you break the math: winners get tiny, stops stay full -1R.

Two valid outputs: stop or target/partials rule. “Scary” is not a rule.

sections.title: Plan: entry → stop → target
sections.caption: The target and R:R are fixed before the order.

sections.body:
Partial fixation at 1R = close half (or ⅓) when the price has passed exactly your risk.
After partial: stop on the balance → at breakeven (entry) or trail according to the structure.

Do not move the stop further from the price “to let it breathe” - this will increase the risk after entry.

sections.title: Part for 1R, remainder to goal
sections.caption: 1R fixed → BE on balance → 2R according to plan.

sections.title: Practice
sections.body: On ETH 15m history (without clicking):
items: Find one profitable idea with a clear stop
Write it down: would you close 50% on 1R? where's the rest?
Write one phrase prohibiting “leaving early out of fear”

title: Entry and exit plan: R:R minimum 1:2
subtitle: Each trade starts with three numbers
outcome: Create a plan template: entry, stop, target, R:R ≥ 1:2 before any click.
content: R:R - risk to reward ratio; 1:2 means earning twice as much as you are willing to lose.
takeaway: No R:R 1:2 - no deal. Exceptions for the journal and statistics only.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
Trade plan = three prices: entry, stop, target.
R = |input − stop|. Reward = |goal − entry|.
R:R = reward ÷ R. Need ≥ 2.0 (record 1:2).

If the goal is less than 2R, the idea is canceled or the stop/entry is revised. Not “I’ll go in and we’ll see.”

sections.title: Reward at least 2 times the risk
sections.caption: Stop is closer to the entry, target is further away - otherwise R:R < 1:2.

sections.body:
1) Where is the entry (limit/condition).
2) Where is the stop and why (beyond swing / beyond range).
3) Where is the goal (next level / 2R).
4) R:R ≥ 1:2? Yes → it is possible. No → pass.
5) Risk% of deposit (usually 0.5–1%).

Exceptions to 1:2 are only if there are statistics in the log that the setup lives at 1:1.5. For a beginner - no exceptions.

sections.title: A plan is more important than a “beautiful candle”
sections.caption: A break without R:R 1:2 is not a plan, but a finger impulse.

title: Market cycles: accumulation, growth, distribution
subtitle: Where are we in the BTC cycle and what does it mean for the plan?
outcome: You will recognize the Wyckoff phases in a simplified way, compare them with HH/HL and not confuse a rollback with a market reversal.
content: The market is cyclical: flat → growth → top → fall → flat again.
takeaway: Trade the phase, not the dream: in accumulation - range, in growth - long pullbacks.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
1) Accumulation - sideways after a fall, big players are gaining.
2) Markup (growth) - HH/HL series, upward trend.
3) Distribution (distribution) - sideways at the top, unloading into the crowd.
4) Markdown (fall) - LH/LL, downward trend.

On BTC 1D/4H the phases are visible more clearly than on 5m. First the phase - then the setup.

sections.title: Cycle: range → trend → range → trend
sections.caption: Accumulation and distribution = range; markup/markdown = trend.

sections.title: What to do in each phase

sections.body:
Accumulation: trade range boundaries or wait for a breakout+retest up. Do not shorten the bottom by eye.
Markup: longs from HL / pullback. Not shorting every pullback is “already expensive.”
Distribution: fewer longs on highs; wait for the structure to be scrapped down.
Markdown: shorts from LH or pause; long “bounce” - only according to plan, small size.

Rollback in markup ≠ cycle change. Change = breakdown of HL and appearance of LH (lesson 32).

sections.title: Markup: buying a pullback
sections.caption: In growth we are looking for HL, not a short top.

outcome: Learn to read BTC.D and enter alts according to the rules, not FOMO.
content: Altseason is a phase when alts grow faster than BTC with falling dominance.

sections.body:
Altseason = capital is rotated from BTC to alts: alts grow faster than BTC (sometimes when BTC is calm).
Indicator: BTC.D (Bitcoin Dominance) - the share of BTC in the capitalization of the crypto market.

Typical altseason background: BTC.D is declining, BTC is stable or growing quietly, ETH and major alts are outperforming HH.

sections.title: BTC.D is falling - attention to alts
sections.caption: Declining dominance often accompanies the strength of alts.

sections.body:
1) BTC is not in an acute dump (15m/1H).
2) BTC.D: reduction or clear breakdown of the growth of dominance.
3) Viola has its own structure (HL / range breakdown), not just “everything green in the tape”.
4) Risk ≤1%, R:R ≥1:2.

FOMO signal “everyone is already in profit” without points 1–2 is a pass.

sections.body:
When BTC drops sharply, alts almost always fall harder (β > 1): liquidity is thinner, crowd stops cascade.

Newbie rule: do not long alts “on a rebound” in the first minutes of a BTC dump. First, defense: close/reduce longs of alts, do not average.

sections.title: BTC is down - alts are steeper down
sections.caption: Correlation in stress → 1. Don't go long versus dump.

sections.title: What can you do instead of “catch a knife”?

sections.body:
1) Flat: wait for BTC 15m to stabilize (less wide red bodies).
2) Short only according to plan (structure breakdown, R:R ≥1:2) - not necessary.
3) Long alt - after BTC has shown HL / dump stop, and alt has its own setup. Not “green candle = reversal”.

The purpose of the dump hour is to save the deposit, not to “win back the market.”

title: How to come back after a big loss
subtitle: Pause, size, magazine, psychology
outcome: Restore discipline and capital after a series of losses or one major loss.
content: A big minus hits the score and the head - recovery begins with a pause.
takeaway: A pause and a smaller size are not weakness, but professional hygiene.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words

Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
Big minus (−3R per day, −5% of the deposit, series of 3+ stops) → trading stop 24–48 hours.
Not “one more deal, I’ll win back.” Revenge almost always makes the hole bigger.

Pause = magazine analysis, sleep, sports. Not new indicators or doubling the size.

sections.title: After the blow - a pause, not a rematch
sections.caption: The score is restored by a process, not by tilt.

sections.body:
First 3–5 trades after the pause: half the usual risk (for example 0.5% → 0.25%).
Only setups A according to the checklist. Limit: 1–2 trades per day.

Return the full size - after you again execute the plan without violations (evaluation in the journal), not after one profitable transaction.

title: Trend change: upward → downward
subtitle: Structure breakdown, LH, retest
outcome: You can recognize a downward trend reversal by a Higher Low break and the appearance of a Lower High.

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Uptrend = Higher High + Higher Low (HH/HL).
Suspicion of a change: the price closes below the last significant HL (structure breakdown).

One touch with a shadow is not enough - you need to close behind HL on your TF (15m/1H). The first pullback without breaking HL is still an uptrend.

sections.title: Breaking the last HL
sections.caption: Close below HL → the structure of the uptrend is questionable.

sections.body:

Training short: from LH or retest broken HL from above→bottom as resistance.
Stop - for LH. The goal is the next LL / ≥2R.

Without LH and without a clear stop, don’t short “because it’s scary up.”

title: Federal Reserve Meeting (FOMC)
subtitle: Trading rules on the day of bet decision
outcome: You will understand how the FOMC moves DXY and BTC, and how not to become a victim of news volatility.
content: The FOMC is a macro event with extreme wicks and slippage.
takeaway: Before publication - flat; after - the reaction according to plan.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

sections.body:
FOMC (rate decision) and CPI - minutes when the spread widens, candles on BTC make wicks in both directions, market orders slip.

Rule: flat 15–30 minutes before the release and 15–30 minutes after (until the first or second 15m reaction candle closes). Don’t “guess the direction a second before the number.”

sections.title: The window around the release is outside the market
sections.caption: 15–30 minutes before and after: no new entries.

sections.title: What to do on the day of the event

sections.body:
Before the window: reduce the size or close training positions if you are not ready for a gap.
In the window: observation only - high/low first reaction, spread.
After the window: trade already the structure (level, retest), not the first tick of the news.

The same rule applies to CPI and other red events on the economic calendar.

title: Scam chats and signal groups
subtitle: Seeds don't shine; VIP signals are often a trap

content: Income guarantee, VIP signals and request for seed/USDT “under management” are red flags.

sections.body:
Confidence and profit screenshots are not a skill.
Typical moves: “x10 guarantee”, closed VIP, urgency “enter now”, request to transfer USDT “under management” or “to the general pool”.
The training shows the setup, risk 0.5–1% and stop. Scam requires trust and translation.

Seed phrase, private key, 2FA code, full API with withdraw - do not enter into any “bot”, “guarantor”, “wallet check”, “Telegram support”.
Exchange support itself does not write first in a personal message asking for a seed.
Lost the seed = lost everything, recovery is impossible.

sections.body:
A paid channel “prepare inputs” without your plan is most often a deferred pump/dump or a referral funnel.
Even an “accurate” signal without a trade size, stop and limit is a lottery.
Rule: someone else's signal does not cancel the risk of 0.5–1% and your journal. No plan, no click.

title: Transfer of assets between exchanges

sections.body:
USDT on Ethereum (ERC20), Tron (TRC20), BSC (BEP20) - different networks.
The address and network at the output must match the deposit network at the reception.
Sent ERC20 to an address that the exchange expects only as TRC20 - the funds often cannot be returned.

sections.title: A network error is more expensive than a commission
sections.caption: TRC20 commission is cheaper than ERC20; network error = risk 100% of the amount.

sections.body:
Some exchanges (and networks like XRP, XLM, sometimes a deposit on CEX) require a memo/tag.
Without a memo, the transfer may go “wrong” inside the exchange.

Checklist:
1) Copy the address from the Deposit screen of the target exchange - not from the chat.
2) The network is the same as that selected on Withdraw.
3) Memo/tag, if the field is shown as required.
4) Test: minimum amount → wait for crediting → then the main amount.
5) Do not send the entire deposit with the first click.

sections.title: Small amount first
sections.caption: Until the test is accepted, do not touch the main volume.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: You need to transfer 1000 USDT to Binance → another exchange using TRC20. First 10 USDT TRC20 with the correct address → arrived → balance 990. ERC20 “because I’m used to it” when depositing TRC20 is the path to loss.

sections.title: Why does a trader need an API?

sections.body:
Read-only key reads balance, orders, history - convenient for journaling and accounting.
Trade - you can send orders using the program.
Withdraw — withdraw coins. For a beginner and for a magazine, withdraw is never needed.
Key leak from withdraw = account emptying.

1) 2FA is enabled on the exchange account.
2) The Enable Withdrawals checkbox is turned off and cannot be turned on “just in case”.
3) For the magazine: Enable Reading (read-only).
4) Trade - only if you consciously write a bot; otherwise off
5) IP whitelist, if the exchange allows it, limit it to your IP.
6) Do not screenshot the key into the chat, do not put it in public Git.
7) Different keys for different programs; old - Delete.
8) The Seed of the wallet does not belong to the API and is still not visible to anyone.

sections.title: Withdraw off = deposit alive when reading is leaked
sections.caption: Read-only maximum merges data; withdraw drains money.

Job:

House:

content: Futures = margin + leverage; beginner - Isolated only; leverage ≠ risk size.
takeaway: Isolated; leverage 3–5x; stop before the entrance; risk 0.5–1%; size from stop.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words

sections.body:
Isolated: The risk is limited by the margin of this position.
Cross: a loss can eat up the entire balance - prohibited at the start.
5x leverage reduces the required margin, but does not change the risk in USDT if the stop is in place.
Without a stop, leverage resolves through liquidation.

sections.title: Risk from stop, not from shoulder
sections.caption: USDT risk = deposit × 0.5–1%; size = risk ÷ |entry−stop|.

sections.body:
The formula is the same as on the spot: risk ÷ stop distance.
Funding is paid periodically for holding a pose - it’s a small thing on the scalp, for a long pose, count it in advance.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 500, risk 1% = 5 USDT. BTC entry, stop −$50 from entry. Size = 5÷50 = 0.1 BTC. 5x leverage does not increase that 5 USDT of risk on a live stop.

content: After a stop, the brain wants a quick return; extra trades and stop shifts finish off the score.
takeaway: After −1R there is a pause; stop not “so that it doesn’t get knocked out”; transaction limit is required.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words

sections.body:
Chain: stop −1R → anger → size↑ → entry without a plan → another −1R…
Signs: “I don’t want a day in the red”, timeframe down to 1m “for accuracy”, second trade immediately after the first.
Antidote: after stop, timer for 30 minutes (or stop for the day). The following size is ≤ regular, not ×2.

sections.title: Revenge breaks math 0.5–1%
sections.caption: Two rematches ×2 sizes = easily -4R per hour.

sections.body:
Stop before entry = setup logic. The transfer “just so it doesn’t get knocked out” turns −1R into −2R…−5R.
Breakeven/trail - only according to the plan rule (for example, after +1R), not out of fear.

Overtrading: > limit of trades of the day, entries out of boredom, “one more” after the goal.
Beginner limit: 2–3 trades/day or 1–2R total risk - whichever comes first.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Deposit 1000, risk 1% = 10 USDT (1R). The stop was triggered (−10). Revenge ×3 without a plan: −30. The result is −40 instead of −10—the price to “win back.”

title: Fibonacci correction
subtitle: 0.382 / 0.5 / 0.618 only in trend
outcome: Create a momentum grid and use 0.382–0.618 as retracement zones.
content: Fib Retracement is a map of probable rollbacks within a trend, not magic in a sideways trend.
takeaway: Only by trend: rollback to 0.382–0.618 + confirmation; Do not trade in Fibo range.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
First the trend: higher high/low (uptrend) or lower (downtrend).
Impulse = strong movement; correction = pullback against momentum.
In the side view, the grid gives false “accurate” levels - skip it.
Tool: Fib Retracement. Uptrend - pull from swing low → swing high; see zones 0.382, 0.5, 0.618 as an area of ​​interest, not an “order line”.

sections.title: Rollback in an uptrend to the Fib zone
sections.caption: Upward impulse → correction to 0.382–0.618 → interest in continuation.

sections.body:
Wait for the reaction in the zone: pin/absorption/retest on your timeframe - don’t buy “because 0.618”.
Stop - behind the zone and beyond the minimum of the correction structure.
The goal is at least to the edge of the impulse (100%) or partial from the previous swing; R:R ≥ 1:1.5.
Position risk 0.5–1% of the deposit.

sections.title: Confirmation with a candle near the zone
sections.caption: The Fib zone + rejection candle is stronger than touching the level “in the air”.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: ETH uptrend: impulse 2800 → 3200. Grid: 0.5 ≈ 3000, 0.618 ≈ 2952. Rollback to 3000, bullish pin at 15m. Entry 3005, stop 2968 (−37$). Risk 1% on deposit 740 = 7.4 USDT → size ≈ 0.2 ETH. Target 1.5R ≈ 3060+.

references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Body = open/close; shadows = high/low.
We look for a pattern only at a pre-marked support/resistance or at a valid Fib zone/structure - not in the middle of the “empty” range.

sections.title: Level patterns
sections.caption: Pin / engulfing / doji have weight in S/R, not in a vacuum.

sections.body:
Pin bar: small body, long shadow towards failure. Bullish pin at support - shadow down (they took out the sellers and closed high).
Absorption: the body of the current candle overlaps the body of the previous one. Bullish at support - an argument for going long according to plan.
Doji: open≈close - indecision. It is not an entrance in itself; you need the next candle in the direction of your plan.

Stop: behind the tail of the pin / behind the absorption extreme. Risk 0.5–1%. The target is the next zone, R:R ≥ 1:1.5.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Support 0.95 at SOL 15m, bullish pin: low 0.942, close 0.952. Entry 0.953, stop 0.940 (−0.013). 1% risk on $650 = $6.5 → size ≈ 500 SOL face value. Doji at the same level without the next bullish candle - you miss.

Right shoulder often prints on lower volume than the head.

After an HH/HL series, an LH + H&S is a strong structure-shift signal.

sections.title: Candlestick example
sections.caption: Shoulders ~58, head ~72, neck ~50, close below neck, target ~28 (H=22).

sections.body:
Trigger: close below the neck (a wick is not enough).
Entry on break or retest of the neck from below.
Stop: above the right shoulder (not above the head — too far).
Target: H = head high − neck under the head; project −H from the neck break.
Intraday: take partials at 1R.

sections.title: Numbers scenario (no click yet)
sections.body:
BTCUSDT 4H.
Shoulders ≈ 65,000, head 68,000, neck 63,000.
H = 68,000 − 63,000 = 5,000.
Close below 63,000 → short; stop above right shoulder ≈ 65,200; target 63,000 − 5,000 = 58,000.
R ≈ 2,200, target 5,000 → R:R ≈ 1:2.3. Do not short the “head” before the neck breaks.

title: Resistance and flip levels
subtitle: High zone; old resistance → support
outcome: Find resistance on the highs and plan to short from the zone or go long after the flip.
content: Resistance is the zone of the hays. After a close above, the zone often becomes support (flip).
takeaway: Short from resistance; long after flip on top. A line without touches is not a level.

Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Resistance is where sellers have already stopped growth.
Need 2+ close highs. Draw a stripe, not a thin line.

Power grows with touch and reaction. One high = not yet level.

sections.title: High resistance
sections.caption: Two+ highs → zone from above. Think short near the zone, stop behind it.

sections.body:
Close of the working TF above the zone = breakout.
Often the price returns from above: the old resistance acts as support - this is a flip.

Long: after close above + retest from above. Stop - under the zone.
Short from resistance: at the zone on top, stop behind the highs.

sections.title: Flip from top to bottom
sections.caption: Close above → retest from above. The zone below is already support.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: ETH 15m: highs 3520 and 3528 → zone 3518–3530. Short from the zone: entry 3515, stop 3540 ($25). Risk 1% at 1000 = 10 USDT → 0.4 ETH. Target 1.5R ≈ 3477. Flip: close 3535, retest 3525 from above, stop 3510, target up the structure.

sections.title: Remember
items: Short at the zone, stop behind it
Retest from above = flip in support

sections.body:
4h (or 1D): where the structure is facing - up, down, flat.
1h: where the entry zone is level, retest, range boundary.
15m: trigger - close, pin, zone absorption.

Don't look for a "new" level from zero to 15m, ignoring 4h.

sections.title: From top to bottom: side → zone → click
sections.caption: 4h specifies the arrow. 1h - where to stand. 15m - when to press.

sections.body:
Don't enter 15m against a 4h direction without an explicit plan.
Explicit plan = countertrend written in advance: zone, stop, target, risk ≤ usual.
“It looks like it will bounce” is not a plan.

title: False breakout
subtitle: Shadow ≠ breakdown; close TF; retest; shakeout
outcome: Distinguish a false breakout from a real one and describe the actions after the trap.
content: A puncture by a shadow is not a puncture. We need a close working TF. After a false one, there is often a return to the zone.
takeaway: Shadow ≠ scrap. Close TF + hold. After a false break, don’t take revenge on the market.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Shadow behind the level = price has been outside.
Breakout = close of the bar of your TF behind the level and hold.

Shakeout: quickly taking out stops with a shadow, then returning inside. The newcomer enters the shadows - often into a trap.

sections.title: Shadow puncture ≠ scrap
sections.caption: High/low outside the zone, close back inside - false breakout.

sections.title: What to do after false break

sections.body:
1) Wait for the TF to close - do not click on the first tick of the level.
2) If close returned inside - false: cancel the chase-breakdown.
3) Often trade back to/from the zone away from the trap.
4) Retest of the real party - only after a confirmed close.

Don’t average “because it’s almost over.”

sections.title: After the trap - to the zone
sections.caption: Reach up + close below resistance → often return down.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: SOL 15m, resistance 148.20–148.60. Shadow high 149.10, close 148.05 - false up. Short after close: entry 147.90, stop 149.30 ($1.40). Deposit 1500, risk 1% = 15 USDT → size ≈ 10.7 SOL. The target is to support 145.80 (≈1.5R). A true breakout would be close ≥148.70 and hold higher.

sections.title: Remember
items: Don't chase on the first tick
After the trap - a plan, not revenge

title: Transaction support
subtitle: Partial on +1R, BE, trail; stop do not expand
outcome: Describe the escort plan: half on 1R, BE, trail; without extension of the foot.
content: Once entered, the risk is only reduced. Partial, breakeven, trail by structure or ATR.
takeaway: On +1R - part in plus, stop in BE. Trail. A stop further from the price is prohibited.

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
R = |input − stop| in price.
When the price gave +1R: close ~50%.
Move the stop balance to breakeven (entry) or slightly into plus after commissions.

This way, one mistake later doesn’t eat up the entire benefit of the first part.

sections.title: Partial → BE → target
sections.caption: 1R: half closed. The remainder is protected by BE. Next is the goal plan.

sections.title: Trail and prohibition to expand the stop

sections.body:
Trail: move the stop behind the structure (beyond the new HL/LH) or ~1×ATR from the price.
The step is only in the direction of reducing risk.

Never move your stop further from the “let it breathe” price. This is a new, greater risk after entry - a separate error.

sections.title: Trail by structure
sections.caption: New HL → stop under it. Not back for the old stop.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: Long entry 3400, stop 3360 → R = 40. +1R = 3440. 50% closed at 3440. Remaining: stop → 3400 (BE). ATR(14) 15m ≈ 25: further trail stop ≈ price −25 or for a new HL. Target 2R = 3480. Pushing the stop back to 3320 “to wait it out” is prohibited.

sections.title: Remember
items: Expansion of stop = drain of discipline

sections.title: Middle of range - no edge
sections.caption: No zone on the left/right - no edge. Wait for the border.

sections.body:
The setup is canceled when the close of the working TF has returned inside the figure / beyond the invalidation point.
Example: we were waiting for a breakout to the top - close again below the border = the breakout did not take place.

After cancellation: flat output, log entry. Not a new “recapture” entrance.

sections.title: Close back = cancel
sections.caption: Moving outside + close inside → setup removed.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
Deposit 2000, daily limit −2% = −40 USDT. Already −1R (−20) and −0.5R (−10) → 10 USDT left. New setup with a risk of 20 USDT - prohibited: daily limit.
Pattern: waiting for a close above 100. Long 100.2, stop 99.4. The next bar is close 99.7 inside - the setup is canceled, exit. Not averaging at 99.5.

title: Double top and double bottom
subtitle: Geometry, neck, measured move, stop beyond the second extremum
outcome: Mark the M/W, count the target from the neck and place a stop behind the second peak/days.
content: Two tops/bottoms in one zone + neck breakout. The goal is the height of the figure. Stop - beyond the second extremum.
takeaway: Entrance after close behind the neck. Stop behind the 2nd peak/bottom. Target = H from neck break.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Double peak (M): two highs at a close height, between them there is a depression - the neck.
Double bottom (W): two layers, between them there is a peak - a neck.

Without the neck, it's just a two-touch, not a figure with a purpose.

sections.title: Double top
sections.caption: Two peaks ≈ one zone. Shorts after close below the neck.

sections.body:
Trigger: close TF behind the neck (down for M, up for W). Often + neck retest.
Stop: behind the second peak (M) or behind the second bottom (W), with a margin under the ATR.
H = |peak − neck| (or |bottom - neck|). Target from neck penetration ≈ H.

Don't enter between peaks "because it looks like M."

sections.title: Double bottom
sections.caption: Two bottoms ≈ one zone. Long after close above the neck.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body: BTC 1H, double top: peaks 69200 and 69150, neck 67800. H = 69200 − 67800 = 1400. Close below 67800 → short 67750, stop 69350 (behind the 2nd peak, ≈$1600). Goal ≈ 67800 − 1400 = 66400. R ≈ 1600; to target ≈ 1350 → R:R about 1:0.85 - weak; wait for a retest of the neck from top to bottom or cut down the size / look for a better R.

items: Entrance after close behind the neck

title: Flag and pennant
subtitle: Pause after the impulse: pole + flag, target ≈ pole height
outcome: Find out the flag/pennant, measure the pole and set a goal ≈ pole height.
content: After a strong move, the price rests in a narrow channel or triangle - this is a continuation, not a reversal.
takeaway: Trade the flag breakout following the trend; target ≈ shaft length; without a staff there is no flag.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
The impulse up or down is the pole.
Then a narrow pause against the impulse: parallel channel = flag, converging triangle = pennant.

Without a strong pole, the “flag” is just noise. The pause is short: usually 5–20 candles, the volume is lower than on the impulse.

sections.title: Impulse → flag → breakdown
sections.caption: Shaft up, slight drift down, close above the top of the flag.

sections.body:
Direction: towards the shaft only.
Trigger: close of a working timeframe beyond the border of the flag/pennant.
Stop: beyond the opposite border of the pause or beyond the last swing inside.
Goal: from the breakdown point, set aside the height of the shaft H ≈ the pulse length.

Retesting the border after a breakout is a calm entry. Without close, there is no signal.

sections.title: Pause channel
sections.caption: Narrow channel after the impulse; breakout along the trend, target ≈ H shaft.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
ETHUSDT 15m. Pulse 3200 → 3320: H = 120.
Flag: rollback 3320 → 3280, channel ~$40. Close above 3310 → long.
Stop 3270. Target ≈ 3310 + 120 = 3430.
Risk 0.5% of deposit. Without H numbers, do not write “flag” in the journal.

sections.title: Remember
items: First the shaft, then a pause
Shorting a flag in an uptrend is a mistake

title: Wedge versus triangle
subtitle: Equal slope of boundaries vs flat side or converging slopes
outcome: Distinguish a wedge from a triangle and call the classic bias rising/falling wedge.
content: Wedge: both borders in one direction. Triangle: one side is flat or slopes towards each other.
takeaway: Rising wedge is often bearish; falling wedge is often bullish - but entry only after close.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Wedge: both lines are inclined in the same direction and converge.
• Rising wedge - highs and lows rise, but highs slower → upward compression.
• Falling wedge - highs and lows fall, lows faster → downward compression.

Triangle:
• one border is almost flat (asc/desc triangle), or
• both converge, but the slopes are in different directions (symmetrical).

Newbie mistake: calling any “corner” a wedge.

sections.title: Rising wedge
sections.caption: Both borders are up, narrowing. Classic: often a breakdown downwards.

sections.body:
Textbook classics: rising wedge → more often than shorts; falling wedge → often long (reversal or continuation after a downtrend).
This is a bias, not an order. Trigger - close abroad towards the plan.

Do not confuse it with the ascending triangle: there the resistance is flat, the lows are rising - this is a different setup, often a bullish breakout.

sections.title: Falling wedge/flat-top comparison
sections.caption: Both borders down = wedge. Flat top = triangle, not wedge.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
BTCUSDT 1H. Rising wedge: low 64000 → 64500 → 64800, high 65500 → 65600 → 65650. Rising wedge. Close below the lower border 64800 → short.
Stop above the last high 65700. Target: the height of the wedge at the beginning ≈ 1500 → zone ~63300.
Risk 0.5%. While close is above the bottom line - just an observation.

sections.title: Remember
items: Wedge: both edges in one direction
Entry after close, not “from the middle”

title: VWAP and daily levels
subtitle: Asia H/L, PDH/PDL and VWAP as intraday average
outcome: Mark Asia high/low, PDH/PDL, VWAP and tie bias longs above VWAP on a trending day.
content: Daily benchmarks: yesterday's high/low, Asia, VWAP. Without indicator farm.
takeaway: Higher VWAP in an uptrend day - long bias; short carefully. Asia/PDH/PDL levels are a map, not a strategy.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
PDH / PDL - yesterday's high and low. Magnets and rebound/breakdown zones.
Asia high / Asia low - range of the Asian session; Mid London often “wins back” these edges.

VWAP is the average price with volume weight from the opening of the day (or session). The price is drawn to VWAP as “fair” within the day.

Enough of these lines. Don't add 5 oscillators.

sections.title: Sessions and levels of the day
sections.caption: Asia → London → NY. On the chart: Asia H/L, PDH/PDL, VWAP line.

sections.body:
Trending day up: the price stays above VWAP - long bias, shorts against the trend - rarely.
Below VWAP in a down day is short bias.

Rebound to VWAP in an uptrend = typical pullback long (if the structure allows).
Closing the day is far from VWAP + return - often mean-reversion, but this is a different story: structure and risk first.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
BTCUSDT 15m. PDH 67500, PDL 66200. Asia high 67000, Asia low 66500. VWAP now 66840. Price 67120 above VWAP after London open, trend of the day is up.
Plan: long from the VWAP zone 66840–66900, stop under Asia low 66480, take to PDH 67500.
Risk 0.5%. Short from 67100 “because it’s high” - against the day and VWAP: pass.

sections.title: Remember
items: Higher VWAP in up-day → long bias

title: Funding, open interest and alt power
subtitle: OI + price, extreme funding and RS alt to BTC
outcome: Read the price/OI/funding link and evaluate the strength of the alt relative to BTC for a signal in the chat.
content: An increase in price with an increase in OI is the same as an increase in short cover. Funding at the extreme is a risk of squeeze.
takeaway: Price↑+OI↑ = trend strength; Price↑+OI↓ = cautious; extreme funding → squeeze risk; alt vs BTC = RS.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
OI (open interest) - how many contracts are still open.
• Price ↑ + OI ↑ - new positions according to the trend: strength.
• Price ↑ + OI ↓ – often short cover: the move may end.
• Price ↓ + OI ↑ – pressure of shorts / longs in a trap.

OI alone is not enough: look along with the price and context of BTC.

sections.title: Strong vs fragile growth
sections.caption: A momentum with rising OI lasts better than a pump with falling OI.

sections.body:
Funding: periodic payment long↔short. Strongly positive funding for a long time = longs are overheated → risk of down squeeze. Strong negative = shorts are overheated → risk of up-squeeze.

RS (relative strength): alt is stronger than BTC if alt % rises more/falls less in the same window.
Long alt, when BTC collapses and the alt is weaker, is usually worse.

For chat: one line of facts (price/OI/funding/RS), then entry plan.

sections.title: Risk of overheating
sections.caption: Extreme funding = not “necessarily a reversal”, but smaller size/harder stops.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
SOLUSDT. Price +4% for 4H, OI +8%, funding +0.04% (moderate). BTC for the same time +1.5% → SOL is stronger than BTC (RS+).
Conclusion: a trend with fuel, not a pure short cover.
Signal idea: long from the structure, risk 0.5%. If funding jumps to +0.15%, we’ll cut the size.

outcome: Collect the signal into one card and mark the setup with the A/B/C tag in the journal.
content: A good signal can be read in 5 seconds. The magazine and chat speak the same language.
takeaway: The template is required; A/B/C tags in the magazine - to compare quality, not "feel".
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson idea in your own words
Post a chat signal: pair, TF, entry, stop, target, invalidation
Log risk % and setup tag in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
One thought format:
Pair TF Side | setup name | Entry Stop Target | Risk% | Invalidation.

Example fields:
• Pair TF Side – BTCUSDT 15m LONG
• Setup - Flag / Flip / VWAP pullback
• Entry Stop Target - numbers, not “about”
• Risk% – 0.5 or 1, not “small”
• Invalidation – which cancels the idea (close, level, BTC against)

Without a stop or without invalidation is not a signal, but an opinion.

sections.title: Plan before click
sections.caption: Entry, stop, take and risk% on one diagram - just like in a chat.

sections.body:
A - full checklist: structure, levels/pattern, risk%, BTC context/session.
B - normal setup, but a hole (weak volume, mid-range, noisy funding).
C - FOMO / "cool" - in the magazine as a pass or micro-size, not as pride.

The tag is placed before the result. Plus on C does not make setup A.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.body:
Signal:
ETHUSDT 15m LONG | Flag after impulse | Entry 3310 Stop 3270 Target 3430 | Risk 0.5% | Invalidation: close below 3270

Journal: tag A (there is a shaft, a close breakout, the risk has been calculated, BTC does not mind).
The same entry without a stop in the chat - we don’t post.
